rescue_reasoning: Keep as climate-relevant Resource Efficiency. Source text directly promotes reuse/refill packaging systems: policy_statement: "incentivizing reusable/refillable solutions across packaged goods sectors"; verbatim_text: "Reusable/Refillable containers... incentivize reusable and refillable solutions... refill at home... return... on the go." This aligns with the mechanism reusable_packaging_incentives → single_use_packaging_waste_reduction (resource efficiency via reduced material throughput and waste). The gate failure cites missing outcome tokens, but the substantive resource-efficiency intervention is explicit. Climate_screen is explicit_self, reinforcing inclusion.
policy_statement: Enact legislation incentivizing reusable/refillable solutions across packaged goods sectors (refill/return at home and on-the-go).
mechanism_description: The State should enact legislation that incentivize reusable and refillable packaging (refill/return models), reducing single-use packaging waste and the emissions associated with producing and disposing of that packaging.
primary_causal_pathway: Same packaging service → ↓ virgin material demand + ↓ manufacturing/disposal energy → ↓ lifecycle GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Shift consumer-goods packaging systems from single-use formats to reusable/refillable (return/refill infrastructure, standardized containers, take-back/cleaning logistics).  
(2) Mediating process: Fewer single-use units are manufactured and discarded, reducing extraction, processing, and manufacturing of plastics/paper/glass/aluminum and reducing waste treatment (landfill/incineration/recycling) demand; reuse cycles amortize impacts over many uses.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions (CO₂e) from materials production and waste management (with possible additional reductions in litter and local pollution as co-benefits).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced lifecycle GHG emissions from avoided production (and disposal) of single-use packaging materials.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Providing the same packaging function with less material throughput reduces energy use across production and disposal, yielding lower lifecycle GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While emissions mitigation is the end result, the defining mechanism is efficiency/circularity (reduced material and waste per unit service), which fits Resource Efficiency better than a direct Mitigation lever (e.g., fuel switching) or Nature-Based Solutions/Adaptation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program’s dominant impact were methane avoidance from diverting biodegradable packaging from landfill, it might look like Mitigation; however, for reusable/refillable packaging the main effect is avoided new packaging production (resource throughput reduction). Transportation/washing emissions can reduce net benefits but do not change the dominant pathway.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that incentivizes reuse/refill to reduce single-use packaging waste inherently reduces material demand and waste management needs, so the Resource Efficiency primary category applies across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: The exact emissions sources (manufacturing vs disposal) and net impact depend on material type, reuse cycles, and logistics, and should be assigned at the individual policy level with supporting evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG benefit can vary with washing/transport intensity, energy mix, and achieved reuse rates; some designs may shift impacts (e.g., heavier reusable containers) or primarily target litter rather than climate, but the dominant mechanism remains reduced material throughput for equivalent service.
key_indicators: “enact legislation”; “incentivize reusable and refillable solutions”; “across the full spectrum of the packaged goods sectors”; “refill at home”; “return from home”; “refill on the go”; “return on the go”
co_benefits: Reduced litter and plastic pollution; potential reductions in local air/water pollution from waste disposal and material production
instance_edge_case_notes: The instrument is described generically (“legislation that incentivize”) without naming the specific tool (e.g., tax credits, grants, deposit-return, EPR eco-modulated fees). If the legislation were primarily deposit-return/take-back requirements, it could shift toward a regulatory-mandate; if it set minimum reuse rates, it could be target-setting/performance-standard.
